speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor of the Shoah, interview September 23, 1994. Survivor, Dr. Gottfried Bloch. Interviewer, Dana Schwartz. In Los Angeles, the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. I do need to--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's start with your name and where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My name is Gottfried Al-- for Albert-- Bloch. B-L-O-C-H. I was born in Teplitz-Schonau, which was a small town in Bohemia, which is now called Teplice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your family and perhaps your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. My grandfather was, was dead. I never met him. He had a factory in the outskirts of the city and died very young, sometime, maybe in his late 40s. My grandmother, the mother of my mother, was a pianist.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birnbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Birnbaum.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helena Birnbaum. She was not professional playing, but was considered a very knowledgeable musician, usually, now when I search in the city. My father's grand-- father was dead. I never met him either. He apparently also died early. And my grandmother was a somewhat strong, quiet lady, which didn't have to give too much. Both these grandmothers died pretty much the same time, when they was-- were transported to Theresientstadt. And two weeks after their arrival, there died in their 80s. My parents, so I didn't meet my father when I was born. He was an officer and was already fighting in Sarajevo in first World War. So I met him the first time, I think, when I was five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the man in uniform, yeah, who played an important role in my analysis later in life, sure. But I don't remember details, of course. I was a seven-month baby and supposed not to survive. Because they had no means at that time. And in, in spite of all, I started to become a survivor the first time. I really shouldn't make jokes about it. But anyway, my father, at that time, had the factory, which he inherited from his father. When he came back, when the war was over, that factory was run down. And he had just to leave it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had they been producing in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They produced underwear. Very successful factory when there was a cold winter, which isn't always. So it went up and down. Now, he was a rather gifted man and knew  he had to start from the beginning. So he went back to school and became a-- accountant-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] public accountant, and started a practice, and supported his family. While he was in school, my mother started a boutique with handicrafts that partially was done by her and by a couple of other women who she employed. And somehow, we got through the times that he did not really make any money. And we had no savings. It was after the war. So the beginning was hard but very comfortable for me as a child. I didn't know much about hardship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe some of the memories you have of your childhood. What was the home like? What was the relationship like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my parents had a very good marriage. It was very comfortable. There was never any kind of screaming or yelling. And I was first and only child. And when I was six-- no, when I was five, my sister was born, who was a lovely little baby. So there no great problems. The city was a city of-- and I can't even tell. Today it seems so little. There were 30,000 or 35,000 people. And yet we had culture. We had a theatre and an opera and, and music. It is hard to believe. There were about 3,000 Jews, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] most of them doing quite well, except for newcomers, who, after first war, came with nothing, from the east, usually, most from Russia or Poland, and started from the beginning, and were somehow supported and made it. Nobody ever starved in the city. I mean, even the beggars had a life that is certainly not comparable with homeless person today. I like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your own parents doing anything with these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that they were very generous in the means available at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way were they generous? What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main interest the newcomers had was in clothes. And I remember one family, their name was Kalb-- K-A-L-B. I went to school with one of the children, who became a doctor that-- who specialized in going to the Jewish families, collecting clothes. He ended up as a second department store, in Teplice because most of the clothes he sold-- and saved money. And so they slowly worked themselves out and became very wealthy, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did these people come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came from, I think, from Galicia, from Poland, which was Austria prior to the war, then became Poland. And I think it was pretty antisemitic at that time in Poland. And they just wanted to get away. They were educated people. And all the kids went into good schools. And a couple of them, to the university.

speaker: interviewer
text: What years are we talking about? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that is the later '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born in 1911.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm just 80 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're talking about the '20s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, later, yeah, middle '20s, my high school time. Was the beginning of my high school time. The elementary school and high school. And I kept and got in--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were as relationships were at that time, respectful. And nothing as our kids do today. They knew very little about me, I think. They were satisfied that I was good in school and liked to read books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a very educated man. He-- his hobby was history. And he had all the history books of the world, and liked when I came with questions. So he would, anyway, talk to me. But it was not an intimate relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a lot of emotional distance then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you take care of yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had friends. Usually somewhat older friends, one year older, two years older. Because I was curious. I wanted to know more than I knew. And I don't think I had too bad a time. School was relatively easy for me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You asked before about antisemitism during [INAUDIBLE]. Of course it existed. But it existed in an-- in a not-so-painful way. People were somewhat more separate. There was a sports club where Jews are not welcome. And there was Maccabees, the Jewish sports club where the Jews were. And of course, they had a kind of [INAUDIBLE] sport contracts. But no open animosities, et cetera. And I really felt more as belonging as I ever had later and belonged at any place. Maybe with the exception of here. But that's a small complicate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you first sense that there was something wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, when Hitler came to power in '33 in Germany-- and there was a border. And I mean, there was Germany. And even though there was a German area where I lived, the schools were German, the people spoke German, my mother tongue was German, it was another country. It was a very democratic country, that Czechoslovak republic that existed for a very limited time, from the end of First World War, '19, until the beginning of a Nazi invasion in '39. It was, for 20 years, a rather democratic ideal.

speaker: interviewee
text: When did I for the first time? I can't tell you. I, I know the day. Not the date, but I see the day. I was skiing in the mountains, which were the border between Germany and Bohemia, that part of Czechoslovakia where I lived. And we never considered a border.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were skiing, as we always have done, we just went over the border and back, and wherever our trails were. And suddenly, came a small corps of SA-- and these were the foreigner of the SS, SA means Sturmabteilung-- in these brown uniforms, and skiers, and surrounded us, two or three of my friends and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And said, do you know where you are? And I said, no. We come up here from Teplice, where we live. 'You are in Germany. And you get out.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the first time that I felt animosity. Before we would go over. We would, later on, buy German cigarettes, which were better than the Czech cigarettes. And we wouldn't find, really, that there was a difference anyway. I knew now that there was a border. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I had a very close friend, whom I the last time visited last year in Germany. We went to high school together. That is already a little later. And he was quite enthusiastic about the changes in Germany. And he said, you know, this is a historical development, more important than anything that has happened for hundreds of years. And I told him what I have heard, that they had concentration camps, that they were beating people there, that Jews had to leave the country. And I hear him. And I know on what sport we were walking from school when he said it. Oh, that is just a childhood disease of a new system. That won't be important. Important is that Germany is getting big and strong again. Now, when I saw him, since last year, when I visited him in Wiesbaden, where he had invited me to stay in his house, he said, oh I cannot, cannot believe this. And he really didn't want to talk about this. I was eager. I was very much interested in how these people look back, now, at this time. But the only way, I guess, they can live with themselves is by denying it or saying it may not have been. And that was an intelligent man, a man who had a doctorate in political science. His wife, who was a much simpler woman, was much more sympathetic. Anyway, he is 80 years old, like I am. And we were very touched to meet each other again. But that area is cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in, in the university at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The university was in Prague, which is the-- was and is the capital of the country. And that univ-- even though it was in the middle of a Czech area, there were two universities. One was a German university, where I was, and the other was the Czech university. And curious enough, there were many Jewish professors in the German university. There were very few Jewish professors in the Czech university prior to 1938. Of course, that, that, was the end of it. That is where I started, in '30-something '33, 1933, on the medical faculty of the German university, to study medicine. And got nearly to the end. And then, 1938, the Germans conquered, in a way-- diplomatically, without any shooting or war action-- the Sudetes, which were the German part of Czechoslovakia. There were England and France that had guaranteed they would help to preserve our country and give up at the last moment. Chamberlain, who was the minister of the exterior at that time, came there and, and sold us out. And Jews had to leave that part or were immediately arrested and transported to concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this German part.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? What was that date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938. It was in Novem-- no, October 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe what happened to you and your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, anyway, in Prague, which was still the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Czech part?

speaker: interviewee
text: So as a Jew of the Czech part, I went to Teplice. I picked up my parents and whatever we could take in a luggage and left with the last train to Prague. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were some unpleasantness. The Czechs wanted to send them back. The German wouldn't take take them or support us. So anyways, they stayed in Prague. But our apartment, our furniture, our possessions, whatever-- we had to leave it in pretty good conditions-- stayed there and was lost. The next month, we all were very busy to see-- to find some [INAUDIBLE] into another country, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was very difficult. First of all, you had to-- this country was just closed. There was a quota of how many visa you could get per year. And I think that was given for the next 10 years. So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any countries who would welcome you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were countries. But you either had to have somebody there who paid enough money that you could get a visa. On the black market, you could get visas to some of the South American countries, but lots of money. We didn't have it. I mean, it was a-- an equivalent of thousands of dollars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, my father found a-- one of his customers who had a factory in the Czech part would hire him. So he got, as accountant, some activities there, some income, and we could survive for the next month. I got a job at the Jewish Community Center. Now, I don't want to go too much into detail. But there was a man, Willy Schoenfeld, who had the idea that if Jews who are now thrown out of their professions-- stores, lawyers, doctors-- want to go into another country, where they don't have licenses, they have to be prepared. And he created training courses for reschooling and reeducation of people as preparation for emigration.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they-- what did they teach there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything, you only can think of it. Now, first of all, there was agricultural book, which was particularly sought for Palestine. That was prior to Israel. Of course, there was a very limited immigration, too, but very helpful. There was watchmaking and clothes repair.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was basically retraining people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And retraining people as they come into a new country, they don't know the language. They don't have a license to be doctors or lawyers or accountants. So I can say, this is what I now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Even with another language that they didn't know, they could do this work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and of course, there were language courses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where was this school? Was it in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague. Of course, it wasn't a real school. It was different places wherever we--

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you-- did you take part in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took place-- a part in one thing, in namely, the selection of people. What we did is we, first of all, interviewed the people, which was as painful, maybe, as this [INAUDIBLE] can become. Because people were talking about what they had and what they lost. We would test people with, with-- to find out what their strengths and gifts and skills were, and then sit with them and, and help them to make some choices.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you already then interested in psychiatry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had already, as a student, done some practical work in psychiatry and neurology.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was right up your alley.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was pretty much my alley, if you would. Yes. He also, Willy Schoenfeld, was a famous graphologist. And I learned on his side, he gave courses in graphology, that skill which became very helpful to me in later life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Graphology being the science of--

speaker: interviewee
text: The science of handwriting analysis. Not only for purpose of identification, but by purpose of reading character, personality traits.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you believe in graphology?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a religion. It's not a matter of believing. I mean, it is a very practical and very helpful thing for people who know-- I know there are many charlatans who just-- who make hocus pocus out of it, and are like psychics, which has nothing to do with it. I mean, I have a library of handouts of very serious books. And have even given courses here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So you were involved in 1939, late '38 and '39, in this way of helping people to retrain themselves in order to escape. And then what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the same time, I was employed. So I had a little income there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that my father had a little income. I had  a little income. And we didn't have to live from social support.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister was there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister at that time, went to Holland, where we had family. Not close family, but somehow cousins of cousins or second-degree cousins who employed her as household help. That was the only way the Dutch government would allow people, immigrants, to work. And so she thought she could survive and basically did at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Needless to say, during that period, I wrote to everybody, to the universities, to find some way to get out. And the only thing I didn't do-- and some people saved their lives by doing this-- was to go in an illegal transport to Palestine. Some of these peoples were-- from-- these transports, died on the way. But quite a number of people, younger people, made these journeys on Turkish miserable boats, and somehow got to Palestine. And some of my friends did it and their wives.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reason for not going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably-- it was a complex situation. First of all, I probably wasn't courageous enough. I knew how dangerous it was. I always did what was right. And an illegal transport didn't seem good to me. It was very limited, but I didn't. Not the first time that I made the decision, this kind of decision. Maybe I didn't want to leave my parents. They couldn't probably go on such a transport. Although I knew other people who did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that went on until-- oh, for some time I was sent to work on a road. I had to do housework and so-- but that was not-- it was still in the framework of coming home at the end of the week or not being deported yet until, until the war started, which was-- I mean, the war had started in '39, until the war expanded.

speaker: interviewee
text: America entered the war. The Japanese entered the war. And emigration was finally and-- cut off. Now it's everything. Nobody gets out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me a date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. It was '41. It was fall of '41. '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: At this moment, I don't exactly remember when America entered the war. But I think it was the end of '41. When was Pearl Harbor? Wasn't it in December of '41?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, I'm pretty sure. Yeah. Now as the situation changed, and everybody wondered what will they do with Jews now? Before, they supported emigration. Just get out. Leave everything you have and get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one day that Willy Schoenfeld met a friend who came from one of the border towns, deported to Poland. And there was a letter, smuggled from Poland to this place-- got in the hands of the Jewish community head, president. And they sent this friend over to Prague to inform us about this letter. And this letter said, there is something terrible going on in Poland. They are building a camp in a small town called Oswiecim, Auschwitz. And they're build-- some-- we have the feeling something is built there that will help them to execute Jews in large amounts. I cannot tell you whether they talked about the gas chamber. But they talked about preparation for mass execution. And they wanted us to know about it and wondered what one can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was one friend. And of course, a very secret, hush-hush meeting. And-- because one never knew if their provocation, where-- wrote this original death letter, we didn't know. Maybe it is just to make us somehow more willing to give everything away we had. Because at that time, already, the situation had become very difficult. Jews had to leave many of their places and, and live together, not in a ghetto, but in restricted conditions. All the stores, of course, the factories, were taken away. And then came something that was as revealing, even though we didn't quite understand it. Maybe in-- it still in '41, during the winter, there came the order Jews had to deliver all pets at a certain place, outside of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had to deliver--

speaker: interviewee
text: All pets. Pets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dogs, cats, birds, horses. Fantastic things that we didn't even know that people would have. They used to have ranches outside of-- in the country. And maybe somebody still saved a pig or a horse. And they wondered what-- what that means, why do I have to give my dog. Doesn't do anything to anybody.
